text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 25 March 2018 

Dr Amelia Clarke
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Clarke
Consultant Neurologist,  

James Patel, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K392174 NHS No. 9056734211  
12 Maple Close, Bromley, BR1 2HT

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in the neurology clinic. He attended with his partner and brought a detailed diary. He has a background history of migraine without aura since adolescence, typically unilateral throbbing headaches with photophobia and phonophobia, occurring about once per month and responsive to simple analgesia and rest. There is no history of head injury, CNS infection, or febrile convulsions. The current clinical focus, however, is his recent seizure recurrence. On Carbamazepine monotherapy he was seizure-free for 8 months, until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago. He reports a brief warning of an internal sense of déjà vu followed by behavioural arrest and loss of awareness, with disorientation lasting around 15–20 minutes afterwards as described by his partner. There have been no witnessed convulsions, tongue biting, or incontinence. He denies nocturnal events and there have been no injuries. Alcohol intake is modest (2–3 units/week) and sleep has been slightly reduced around work deadlines.

Examination today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular and systemic examinations were normal. Neurological examination revealed no focal deficits; gait was steady and fundi were normal to direct ophthalmoscopy. There were no signs of cognitive impairment and mood appeared stable. He is fully independent in activities of daily living and continues office-based employment.

Investigations to date include a routine EEG performed last year reported as within normal limits, and a brain MRI with epilepsy protocol six months ago showing no structural abnormality. Baseline blood tests (U&E, LFT, FBC) from his GP last month were within reference ranges; carbamazepine trough level was not checked at the time.

In discussion, he feels that the prolonged seizure-free period had given him confidence to continue driving for work purposes and he is understandably anxious about the implication of this recent event. He also noted increased occupational stress in the fortnight preceding the relapse and two nights of curtailed sleep.

Plan discussed with the patient:
- Check carbamazepine trough level to confirm adherence and therapeutic range.
- Repeat routine EEG; consider sleep-deprived EEG depending on results and diary correlation.
- Safety counselling reinforced regarding seizure first aid, hydration, and sleep hygiene; we provided written information.
- He has self-reported cessation of driving since the event; I advised that he should continue to follow DVLA guidance and we will provide a clinic summary for his records.
- Continue current carbamazepine dose pending levels and EEG; we will review for any dose adjustment once results are available and after discussion with the patient.

I will write again once the results are back and will plan to review him in clinic in approximately 8–10 weeks, sooner if there are further episodes or concerns. Thank you for your referral and for sharing his background migraine history; at present the headaches remain infrequent and manageable, and the priority remains the seizure evaluation.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: On Carbamazepine monotherapy he was seizure-free for 8 months, until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states the patient had been seizure-free for 8 months on carbamazepine until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago. There is a clear single seizure after an 8-month seizure-free interval. Current status: not seizure-free for ≥6 months anymore, and there is 1 seizure over the past 8 months. No indication of additional seizures in that period. Therefore, normalize to '1 per 8 month'.
